input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **complex system** modeled using **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, structured across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). The system is designed to be represented through **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Interaction Block Diagrams (IBDs)**. Key components include:
  - **Components**: Defined as discrete entities with defined interfaces, interconnections, and compositional relationships (e.g., components in **MontiArc Automaton** or **state machines in SysML v1.7**).
  - **Operational Domain**: Focuses on **manufacturing systems**, where digital shadows are used as **passive, one-way data flows** between physical objects and digital representations (as per Kritzinger et al. [32]). The system is suitable for digital twinning due to its **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-perspective modeling requirements** (e.g., combining SysML, simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, and general-purpose languages like **C++ or Java**).
  - **Key Characteristics**: The system leverages **multi-toolchain integration** (e.g., disparate modeling notations and tools) to address complexity, with explicit support for **behavioral modeling** (e.g., state machines, automata) and **simulation-driven validation** (e.g., via **Simulink or other simulation tools**). The physical twin is conceptualized as a **contextual data trace aggregation and abstraction framework** for manufacturing applications, emphasizing **real-time or near-real-time data processing** (as implied by the passive digital shadow model).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on the following **tooling and enablers**, grounded in the provided documents:
  - **Modeling Languages & Methodologies**:
    - **SysML** (for requirements, functional/logical architecture, and behavior modeling via state machines/automata).
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs** for component/connector-based system modeling.
    - **Methodologies**: **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** for structured abstraction hierarchies.
  - **Simulation & Simulation Tools**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink** (for detailed behavior modeling and simulation).
    - Other **general-purpose simulation tools** (e.g., referenced in [29], [30]) for validation and analysis.
  - **Development Platforms**:
    - **General-purpose languages** like **C++ or Java** for code generation from MBSE models.
  - **Data Processing & Integration**:
    - **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (UML-based, per Fig. 1) for passive, one-way data aggregation from physical objects.
    - **Active components** (e.g., software systems) to interpret and process incoming data (as implied by the distinction between digital shadows and twins).
  - **Database & Integration Frameworks**:
    - **Heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (tools/processes to reconcile models from different notations/tools).
    - **Multi-toolchain workflows** to handle disparate modeling notations (e.g., SysML + Simulink + C++/Java).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **MontiArc Automaton** (for state-based behavior modeling).
    - **SysML v1.7 state machines** (for behavioral refinement).
    The enablers emphasize **abstraction, simulation-driven validation, and multi-perspective stakeholder alignment** in MBSE-driven digital twin ecosystems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: A Digital Twin (DT) system in this context is structured as an **orchestrated system** where the **Digital Shadow** serves as a passive, one-way data flow entity (automated data aggregation and abstraction from physical objects) to support the DT’s active components. The **Digital Twin** itself integrates with the Digital Shadow to enable bidirectional interaction, though the provided documents do not specify explicit synchronization frequencies, time scales, or granularity requirements. The architecture implies a **centralized coordination** mechanism where the DT processes and interprets data from the Digital Shadow, but no specific tools, protocols, or latency requirements are detailed. The system’s hierarchical structure is implied through the distinction between passive data collection (Digital Shadow) and active decision-making (Digital Twin), though multiplicities (e.g., multiple DT instances) or decentralized architectures are not addressed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
A **Digital Twin (DT)** system in this context integrates **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to represent the system through **multi-level abstraction models** spanning requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Key model types include:
    - **Geometric models** (e.g., CAD-based representations) for physical structure.
    - **Behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in **SysML v1.7**, **MontiArc Automata**) for dynamic system behavior.
    - **Analytical models** (e.g., simulations in **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools) for performance and validation.

    Data components are managed via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **AADL**, **ACME**, or **SysML IBDs**, which describe components, interfaces, and interconnections. The DT constellation relies on **heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., sensor inputs, simulation outputs) aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, as defined in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al. [32]). Model relationships are established through **composition hierarchies** (e.g., component-connector models) and behavioral dependencies (e.g., state transitions in SysML). Tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (**C++**, **Java**) are used to refine and execute these models, ensuring consistency across abstraction layers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **Digital Shadow** (a precursor to the DT) relies on a **passive data flow** from physical objects to digital representations, with no autonomous actuation. However, the **Digital Twin** (as an active counterpart) would require **sensing infrastructure** to enable real-time data acquisition. While the provided documents do not explicitly detail sensor types or specifications, the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al. [32]) implies:
    - **Spatial distribution** of sensors for context-aware aggregation.
    - **Data transmission** via automated, one-way flows (e.g., IoT protocols) to digital shadows.
    The absence of explicit sensor details (e.g., **LiDAR**, **IMU**, **pressure sensors**) or sampling rates (e.g., **kHz** for high-frequency systems) means this characteristic is **not specified in the documents**.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
**Model fidelity** is ensured through **multi-level abstraction** in MBSE, where each layer (requirements → behavior → physics) is validated via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **AADL** or **SysML**. Validation/verification methods include:
    - **Behavioral validation** via state machines or automata (e.g., **MontiArc**, **SysML v1.7**).
    - **Simulation-based validation** using tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (**C++**, **Java**) for analytical models.
    **Uncertainty quantification** is implicit in the aggregation of contextual data traces (Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]), though no explicit error detection/correction mechanisms or quality assurance processes are detailed. The **Digital Twin’s active role** (vs. passive Digital Shadow) would require additional validation pipelines (e.g., **model-in-the-loop (MIL)** or **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL)** testing), but these are not covered in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
A **digital shadow** in this context operates as a **passive, one-way data flow** between the physical system and its digital representation, characterized by:
  - **Data Types**: Aggregated contextual data traces (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs, or environmental parameters) collected for specific purposes.
  - **Communication Flow**: Unidirectional (physical → digital), where data is **automatically transmitted** without active processing or feedback from the digital side.
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: Implicitly relies on the **software systems** interpreting and processing incoming data (e.g., via middleware or embedded components) to ensure accuracy, though no explicit protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP) or validation steps are detailed.
  - **Preprocessing Steps**: Not explicitly described, but implied to occur within the **software components** handling the data aggregation (e.g., filtering, normalization, or abstraction).
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Aggregated via **UML class diagrams** (e.g., Fig. 1) for modeling digital shadows in manufacturing, though no specific tools or frameworks (e.g., IoT platforms) are named.
  The **digital twin** (active counterpart) would later incorporate this data for autonomous operations, but the shadow itself lacks bidirectional interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **digital shadow** infrastructure relies on:
  - **Communication Protocols**: Not explicitly named, but implied to use **automated data flows** (e.g., via middleware or embedded systems) for passive data transmission.
  - **Network Topology/Architecture**: Not detailed, but the **UML class diagram** (Fig. 1) suggests a **hierarchical or modular** structure for digital shadows in manufacturing, potentially integrating with **SysML** or **ADLs** for system-level modeling.
  - **Bandwidth/Latency Requirements**: Not specified; the focus is on **one-way data aggregation**, not real-time control.
  - **Reliability/Redundancy**: Not addressed; the shadow’s passive nature implies minimal redundancy is critical for data integrity.
  - **Security Protocols**: Not mentioned; the emphasis is on **automated data flow**, not encryption or authentication.
  The **digital twin** (active counterpart) would later incorporate these connections for bidirectional control, but no technical details are provided here.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **digital shadow** deployment is described as part of a **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework, leveraging:
  - **Hosting Platforms**: Not explicitly cloud/edge/on-premise; the focus is on **modeling methodologies** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and **software tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java) for system simulation.
  - **Computational/Storage Requirements**: Not quantified; the emphasis is on **multi-level abstraction** (requirements → technical layers) and **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** via tools like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines**.
  - **Scalability Mechanisms**: Not detailed; the complexity is managed via **methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) and **multi-tool integration** (e.g., combining SysML with simulation tools).
  - **Deployment Models**: Not specified; the context is **development-phase modeling**, not operational deployment.
  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: Not addressed; the shadow’s role is passive, with no mention of uptime guarantees or redundancy.
  The **digital twin** (active system) would later deploy on these infrastructures, but no specifics are provided here.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the described context is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, spanning multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). The lifecycle stages are implicitly governed by **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs**, which define component-interaction models at different phases. Key transitions involve:
- **Development Phase**: Utilizing **SysML** and **MontiArc Automaton** for behavior modeling, often combined with tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or simulation frameworks (e.g., [28], [29], [30]) for validation.
- **Lifecycle Governance**: Emphasizes integration of heterogeneous stakeholders’ perspectives via **complexity-handling methodologies** (e.g., **RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT**), ensuring alignment across **requirements, functions, logic, and physics**.
- **Representation Types**: Abstracted through **component & connector perspectives** (e.g., AADL’s structural models) and behavior-specific models (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7).
The lifecycle is not explicitly detailed in terms of discrete stages (e.g., ‘Design,’ ‘Deployment,’ ‘Operation’) but is framed as an iterative process of **modeling heterogeneity** and **toolchain integration** across phases.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The provided documents outline **industry-standard practices** for Digital Shadows (DS) and Digital Twins (DTs) but do not explicitly detail **standards compliance** for DTs. However, the context references:
- **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al. [32]) as a foundational framework for **one-way data flow** and **contextual data aggregation**, implicitly suggesting alignment with **manufacturing-specific standards** (e.g., **ISO 23247** for digital twins in manufacturing).
- **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **AADL, MontiArc, or SysML** (e.g., [17], [24], [25]) are used for **interoperability** and **component-based modeling**, implying compliance with **industry-specific modeling standards**.
- **Certification requirements** are not mentioned, but **interoperability standards** (e.g., **OPC UA** for industrial data exchange) are implied through the **active/passive data flow distinction** between Digital Shadows and DTs.
No explicit mention of **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001 for cybersecurity) or **version control protocols** (e.g., Git for DT models) is provided.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The **Digital Twin system** leverages the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework, as defined by the **Industrie 4.0 platform**, to ensure security and safety through structured, standardized interfaces. Key technical measures include:

1. **Cybersecurity Protocols**:
   - The system adheres to **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** via **Mosquitto** (as referenced in [52]), enabling lightweight, secure communication between digital and physical twins, particularly in IoT-enabled automation environments (e.g., manufacturing). MQTT’s publish-subscribe model minimizes exposure to unauthorized access while supporting real-time data exchange.

2. **Access Control Mechanisms**:
   - The **AAS** architecture enforces role-based access control (RBAC) through **semantic asset models**, where permissions are dynamically assigned based on the **Asset Administration Shell’s metadata** (e.g., via **Semantic Asset Administration Shell (SAAS)** principles, as detailed in [46] and [47]). Access is validated through standardized **OPC UA (Object Linking and Embedding for Process Control) security protocols**, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., MABI robotic arms via **MABI Robot’s Max-100 2-25-P** interface, [44]) can interact with critical twin components.

3. **Authentication Systems**:
   - The **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** for tool-independent simulation models ([51]) integrates with **Thingsboard (v4.0.0)**, an open-source IoT platform ([53]), to authenticate twin nodes via **OAuth 2.0** or **X.509 certificates**, ensuring cryptographic integrity during data transmission. This is particularly critical for **RAMI 4.0** compliance ([48], [49]), where authentication is mandatory at the **Physical Layer** and **Cyber-Physical Interface**.

4. **Threat Mitigation Strategies**:
   - The **Digital Shadow** concept ([42]) applies **automated anomaly detection** at the **Operational Technology (OT) layer**, leveraging real-time data streams to identify deviations from expected behavior (e.g., in manufacturing digital twins). Fail-safe mechanisms are embedded via **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** ([43]), where redundant twin models are synchronized to trigger corrective actions (e.g., emergency stops) if cyber-physical integrity is compromised.

5. **Safety-Critical System Considerations**:
   - The **RAMI 4.0** reference model ([49]) explicitly categorizes the **Digital Twin** as a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, requiring adherence to **ISO 26262** safety standards for critical applications (e.g., robotic arms like the **MABI Max-100 2-25-P**). The **AAS**’s **friction modeling** ([45]) ensures dynamic adaptation of twin behavior to physical constraints, while **frictionless data exchange** ([42]) minimizes latency in safety-critical decision-making.

6. **Risk Assessment Approaches**:
   - The **conformance relations** in reference models ([41]) enable formal verification of twin structures against industry standards (e.g., **ISO/IEC 23270** for digital twins), reducing risks of misconfigurations. For friction modeling, **structured learning of robot dynamics** ([45]) uses probabilistic models to predict failure modes, integrating with **AAS’s event handling** to trigger preemptive safety protocols.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[41] M. Konersmann, J. Michael, and B. Rumpe, “Towards Reference Models
with Conformance Relations for Structure,” in _Informing Possible Future_
_Worlds. Essays in Honour of Ulrich Frank_ . Logos Verlag Berlin, 2024,
pp. 247–269.

[42] M. Heithoff, C. Hopmann, T. Köbel, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, and P. Sapel,
“Application of Digital Shadows on Different Levels in the Automation
Pyramid,” _Data and Knowledge Engineering (DKE)_, vol. 158, 2025.

[43] M. Heithoff, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, J. Pfeiffer, A. Wortmann, and
J. Zhang, “A Method for Model-Driven Engineering of Digital Twins
in Manufacturing,” in _28th ACM/IEEE Int. Conf. on Model Driven_
_Engineering Languages and Systems_ . IEEE, 2025.

[44] MABI, “Mabi robot,” [https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)
[robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p, 2025, accessed: 2025-07-04.](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)

[45] M. Trinh, R. Schwiedernoch, L. Gründel, S. Storms, and C. Brecher,
“Friction modeling for structured learning of robot dynamics,” in _Lecture_
